perturbation.original_text: CI means Discloser’s non -public written info rmation, in any form, and all copies, summaries and extracts which is identified in writing as confidential at the time of disclosure, and any information disclosed in non- tangible form that is identified as confidential at the time of disclosure and summ arized in a writing labelled as “confidential” delivered to Recipient within 15 days after disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: CI means Discloser’s non -public written information, in any form, and all copies, summaries and extracts which is identified in writing as confidential at the time of disclosure, and any information disclosed in non- tangible form that is identified as confidential at the time of disclosure and summarized in a writing labelled as “confidential” delivered to Recipient within 45 days after disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Extended the deadline for labeling non-tangible information as confidential from 15 to 45 days.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California law requires trade secrets to be actively protected, and a delay this long could jeopardize trade secret status.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts California trade secret law by providing an unreasonably long period to designate orally disclosed information as confidential. Under the UTSA, reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy are required, and a 45-day delay in labeling could be deemed unreasonable, potentially jeopardizing trade secret protection.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the deadline for labeling non-tangible information as confidential from 15 to 45 days. The Law Explanation suggests that this extension could be deemed unreasonable, potentially jeopardizing trade secret protection under the UTSA, which requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not explicitly specify time frames, it refers to 'duty to maintain secrecy', suggesting that a delay of 45 days could be seen as a failure to uphold this duty, thereby creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Either party may terminate this NDA upon 10 days ’ written notice . However, all CI provided under this NDA will remain subject to the terms of this NDA.
perturbation.changed_text: Either party may terminate this NDA upon 2 days’ written notice. However, all CI provided under this NDA will remain subject to the terms of this NDA.
perturbation.explanation: Reduced the termination notice period from 10 days to 2 days.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California law favors reasonable notice periods, and 2 days may be deemed unreasonable, especially if significant confidential information has been exchanged.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification contradicts California Civil Code § 1657, which implies that contractual obligations must be performed within a reasonable time if no specific time is specified. A 2-day notice period for terminating an NDA, especially where substantial confidential information is involved, may be deemed an unreasonably short timeframe, making the termination clause potentially unenforceable under California law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1657. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. INTERPRETATION OF CONTRACTS [1635 - 1663] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) 1657. If no time is specified for the performance of an act required to be performed, a reasonable time is allowed. If the act is in its nature capable of being done instantly—as, for example, if it consists in the payment of money only—it must be performed immediately upon the thing to be done being exactly ascertained. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the termination notice period from 10 days to 2 days. The Law Explanation suggests that this shorter timeframe might be deemed unreasonably short, potentially rendering the termination clause unenforceable under California law. Scraped Law Snippet 1, California Civil Code § 1657, states that if no time is specified for performance, a reasonable time is allowed. While the contract does specify a time, the reduction to 2 days could be viewed as unreasonable, thereby contradicting the requirement of a reasonable time as implied by the law. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition, where a time-sensitive obligation in a contract does not align with legal requirements.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient will stop use of and return or destroy all tangible CI promptly upon reques t, together with any copies, except as otherwise required by law.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient will stop use of and return or destroy all tangible CI within 180 days of request, together with any copies, except as otherwise required by law.
perturbation.explanation: Extended the deadline for returning or destroying confidential information to 180 days.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California law requires reasonable efforts to protect trade secrets, which includes prompt action upon request to return or destroy confidential information. 180 days is not prompt.
perturbation.law_explanation: By extending the deadline for returning or destroying tangible confidential information to 180 days after a request, the modified text potentially contradicts California's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), specifically Cal. Civ. Code § 3426.1. The requirement to protect trade secrets necessitates making reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy, which may include acting promptly to ensure the information is no longer accessible to the recipient when requested. A 180-day delay may be deemed unreasonable, therefore conflicting with state law.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a 180-day delay for the return or destruction of tangible confidential information, which potentially contradicts the obligation to protect trade secrets under California's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA). Cal. Civ. Code § 3426.1 defines 'misappropriation' to include the disclosure or use of a trade secret without express or implied consent, especially if the knowledge was acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use. The original text requires prompt action, aligning with the duty to maintain secrecy. However, a 180-day delay could be deemed unreasonable, thus conflicting with the obligation to protect trade secrets in a timely manner.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient will treat all CI with at least the same degree of care as it treats its own information of similar sensitivity, but never with less than reasonable care.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient will treat all CI with the same degree of care as it treats its lowest priority, non-sensitive information.
perturbation.explanation: Lowered the standard of care for protecting confidential information to the level of 'lowest priority, non-sensitive information'.
perturbation.contradicted_law: This violates the duty to protect trade secrets under California law, as it does not constitute reasonable effort to maintain secrecy.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts the standard of care expected to protect trade secrets under California Civil Code § 3426.1. Trade secret protection requires 'efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy'. Treating confidential information with the same low level of care as non-sensitive data does not meet that standard, therefore violating state law.

(b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The changed text creates a legal contradiction by lowering the standard of care for confidential information to the level of 'lowest priority, non-sensitive information.' This directly contradicts the requirement under California Civil Code § 3426.1 for 'efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy'. The scraped snippet of California Civil Code § 3426.1 defines 'improper means' which includes breaches of the duty to maintain secrecy, implying a standard of care exceeding that of non-sensitive information.

perturbation.original_text: This NDA contains the complete agreement between the parties as to the subject matter, and replaces and supersedes any other communications, or agreements, whether oral or written concerning the subje ct matter. Regardless of conflict of laws principles, this NDA is governed by and construed in accordance with the substantive laws in force in the State of California.
perturbation.changed_text: This NDA contains the complete agreement between the parties as to the subject matter, and replaces and supersedes any other communications, or agreements, whether oral or written concerning the subje ct matter. Regardless of conflict of laws principles, this NDA is governed by and construed in accordance with the substantive laws in force in the State of Nevada.
perturbation.explanation: Changed the governing law from California to Nevada, even though both parties are located in California.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California courts generally enforce choice-of-law provisions, but only if there is a substantial relationship to the chosen state or a reasonable basis for the choice. Neither condition is met, and California has a materially greater interest in the dispute, due to the fact that both companies reside in California.
perturbation.law_citation: Nedlloyd Lines B.V. v. Superior Court, 3 Cal. 4th 459 (1992)
perturbation.law_explanation: By specifying Nevada law to govern an agreement between two California-based entities, the modified text contradicts established California choice-of-law principles. The California Supreme Court's decision in Nedlloyd Lines B.V. v. Superior Court, 3 Cal. 4th 459 (1992), sets the precedent that a choice-of-law provision will only be enforced if the chosen state has a substantial relationship to the parties or the transaction, or there is a reasonable basis for the choice. Because the companies are based in California, and the agreement was made there, California law has a materially greater interest in the case than Nevada law. Therefore, the choice-of-law clause to Nevada may be unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: a judgment against the Dearings in excess of the policy limits, it refused to adjust the matter, stating that it would only be willing to offer the policy limits if it were judicially determined that the policy did in fact provide coverage. fn. 1 Despite defendant's efforts to expedite the declaratory relief action, the personal injury action ultimately went to judgment first, resulting in an award against the Dearings of $33,889.30, a sum far in excess of the insurance policy limits. In the declaratory relief action, although the trial court initially ruled in favor of defendant on the coverage issue, that [15 Cal. 3d 14] determination was subsequently reversed on appeal. (Cal. State Auto. Assn. Inter-Ins. Bureau v. Dearing, supra, 259 Cal. App. 2d 717 .) Thus, a final judgment holding that the policy did extend coverage to the Dearings was entered only on September 5, 1968, over four years after the initial filing of the action. The insurer subsequently paid Ms. Johansen $19,692.19. Since interest on the judgment in the personal injury suit accounted for $8,302.89 of this sum, an outstanding judgment of $22,500 remained against Gary Dearing. Thereafter, Gary Dearing assigned his rights against the insurer to Ms. Johansen in exchange for her promise to release him from personal liability on the outstanding judgment. Ms. Johansen then, as assignee of Gary Dearing, commenced the instant action against the insurer to collect the unpaid portion of her judgment. The trial court, sitting without a jury, rendered judgment in favor of defendant insurer, concluding, in essence, that the insurer's liability could only be predicated upon a finding that its denial of coverage emanated from bad faith, and that in this instance the insurer entertained a bona fide belief that coverage did not exist. fn. 2 In analyzing this case, we note at the outset that the rights at issue here are those of Gary Dearing, the insured; plaintiff Johansen comes before us only as his assignee. fn. 3 Thus, our inquiry necessarily focusses on the nature of the relationship between the defendant insurer and its insured, Dearing, and the nature and scope of the resultant obligations. In Comunale v. Traders & General Ins. Co., supra, 50 Cal. 2d 654 , 658, this court, in a unanimous decision, held that "there is an implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract that neither party will do anything which will injure the right of the other to receive the benefits of the agreement," and that this principle is "applicable to policies of insurance." The implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing imposes a duty on the insurer to settle a claim against its insured [15 Cal. 3d 15] within policy limits whenever there is a substantial likelihood of a recovery in excess of those limits. (Id., at p. 659.) In Comunale, two pedestrians (the Comunales) were struck and injured by a truck driven by Percy Sloan. At the time of the accident, Sloan was insured by defendant Traders & General Insurance Company under a policy that contained limits of liability in the sum of $10,000 for each person injured and $20,000 for each accident. When the Comunales filed suit against Sloan, defendant insurer refused to assume his defense; during the course of the litigation it rejected the Comunales' offer to settle their claims for $4,000, maintaining that the accident did not fall within the policy's coverage. The action proceeded to trial and resulted in a judgment of $26,250 against Sloan. In a subsequent action the court determined that the policy did in fact cover the accident; the Comunales recovered $11,250 from the insurer, the amount of the judgment which fell within the insurance policy limits. Sloan then assigned his rights against the defendant insurer to Comunale who sued the insurer to recover the unpaid portion of his judgment. This court held that the defendant insurer had breached its implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing when, despite the great ris
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the governing law of the NDA from California to Nevada, despite both parties being located in California. The Law Explanation states this contradicts California choice-of-law principles, citing the precedent set in Nedlloyd Lines B.V. v. Superior Court. The explanation clarifies that a choice-of-law provision is enforceable only if the chosen state has a substantial relationship to the parties or transaction. Since both companies and the agreement are based in California, Nevada law's selection may be unenforceable. Scraped Law Snippet 1, while not directly on choice-of-law, illustrates California courts' scrutiny of contractual obligations and the implied covenant of good faith. This supports the assessment that a significant deviation from established legal principles within California constitutes a contradiction.
